source_paper_title: Monotone Causality in Opportunistically Stochastic Shortest Path Problems
context:
Section 6 (Conclusions), page 33 (PDF page 33/35 in the provided parsed text)

Another interesting direction for future work is to find criteria for applicability of label-setting to “stochastic shortest path games,” in which the probability distribution over the successor nodes at each stage depends on the actions chosen by two antagonistic players [46]. We note that some results on the applicability of Dijkstra’s method to deterministic games on graphs have been developed in the last ten years [7, 11], but the case of general stochastic games on graphs remains open.
title: Label-setting (Dijkstra/Dial-type) solvability criteria for stochastic shortest path games
latex:
Given a finite set of states (nodes) \(X=\{1,\dots,n\}\cup\{t\}\), where \(t\) is an absorbing terminal state, consider a two-player zero-sum stochastic shortest path game defined as follows.

- At each nonterminal state \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\), player 1 (the minimizer) chooses an action \(a\in A_i\) and player 2 (the maximizer) chooses an action \(b\in B_i\), where \(A_i\) and \(B_i\) are nonempty compact action sets.
- The stage cost is \(C_i(a,b)\in(0,\infty)\), and the next state \(j\in X\) is drawn according to transition probabilities \(p_{ij}(a,b)\), where \(p_{ij}(a,b)\ge 0\) and \(\sum_{j\in X} p_{ij}(a,b)=1\).
- The terminal state satisfies \(p_{tt}(a,b)=1\) and \(C_t(a,b)=0\) for all actions.

A (stationary) strategy pair \((\mu,\nu)\) consists of measurable maps \(\mu(i)\in A_i\) and \(\nu(i)\in B_i\) for all \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\). For a starting state \(i\), let \(J(i;\mu,\nu)\) denote the expected total cost accumulated until absorption in \(t\) when both players use \((\mu,\nu)\).

Define the value function \(U:X\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) by
\[
U(t)=0,\qquad U(i)=\inf_{\mu}\,\sup_{\nu}\, J(i;\mu,\nu),\quad i\in\{1,\dots,n\}.
\]
Equivalently, under standard assumptions ensuring existence of the value, \(U\) satisfies the dynamic programming (Shapley) equations
\[
U(i)=\min_{a\in A_i}\max_{b\in B_i}\Bigl(C_i(a,b)+\sum_{j\in X} p_{ij}(a,b)\,U(j)\Bigr),\quad i=1,\dots,n,\qquad U(t)=0.
\]

Problem: Find verifiable (a priori, local) sufficient conditions on the game data \(\{A_i,B_i,C_i,p_{ij}\}\) that guarantee the applicability and correctness of non-iterative label-setting algorithms (in the spirit of Dijkstra’s or Dial’s methods) to compute \(U\) on \(X\). In particular, identify conditions ensuring an appropriate monotone causality property (i.e., that under optimal stationary strategies, every state \(i\) depends only on successor states \(j\) with strictly smaller values \(U(j)<U(i)\), possibly with a uniform gap \(U(j)\le U(i)-\delta\) for some \(\delta>0\)) so that a single-pass label-setting computation is valid.
